retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Click here for a simplified version. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Although cCMV can be diagnosed prenatally, this condition is most often diagnosed after birth. Prenatal diagnosis involves maternal and fetal diagnostic studies.

retrieved_docs.title: Antepartum Infections -- Epidemiology -- Cytomegalovirus
retrieved_docs.content: Primary infection with CMV is usually asymptomatic, making the diagnosis in pregnant women difficult. Symptoms can include a prolonged febrile illness resembling mononucleosis, sore throat, rash, and transaminitis. Cerebral manifestations of congenital CMV infection include periventricular calcifications, microcephaly, parenchymal echogenic foci, cerebral pseudocysts, hypoplastic corpus callosum, and abnormal gyri. These findings are often visible on fetal ultrasound before delivery. [31] Common systemic manifestations in neonates include intrauterine growth restriction (IUGR), thrombocytopenic purpura, microcephaly, hepatosplenomegaly, and pneumonia. Long-term complications are common, with up to 90% of symptomatic infants and 5% to 15% of asymptomatic cases developing conditions such as hearing impairment, neurological abnormalities (including structural brain defects, hydrocephalus, and seizures), and developmental delays. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of severe morbidity is increased due to central nervous system damage, leading to neurodevelopmental delays, hearing loss, and vision impairment. Asymptomatic disease is not entirely benign, as 10% to 15% go on to develop long-term morbidities. [4] [5] Despite these risks, clinician awareness of cCMV among women of reproductive age is much more limited than for congenital toxoplasmosis or Down syndrome, which occur at a lower incidence. [6] [7] [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Evaluation -- Fetal and Newborn Cytomegalovirus Evaluation
retrieved_docs.content: Because CMV may be present in breast milk, infant specimen collection for urine or saliva testing should occur at least 1 hour after breastfeeding to prevent contamination. [37] Recommended laboratory assessments include a complete blood count, liver enzyme levels, total and direct bilirubin measurements, and cranial imaging, starting with ultrasound and followed by magnetic resonance imaging if necessary. [30] Although a 2017 informal recommendation by the International Congenital Cytomegalovirus Recommendations Group supported the use of saliva as a diagnostic specimen, its lower specificity compared to urine increases the likelihood of false-positive PCR results. [38]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: When CMV infects the placenta, it impairs trophoblast function, leading to placental insufficiency and hypoxia, which can cause intrauterine growth restriction. [21] CMV infects neural progenitor cells in the developing fetal brain, especially in the ventricular and subventricular zones. This disrupts their growth, differentiation, and migration, leading to neuronal cell loss, impaired cortical development, and the development of structural brain abnormalities, including cortical atrophy, ventriculomegaly, and microcephaly. [22] Hearing loss, the most common complication, results from direct viral injury to cochlear structures, immune-mediated inflammation and neuronal loss, and correlates with the extent of early viral load and inflammation, rather than viral presence at later stages. [23]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: The risk of long-term neurological consequences is high in symptomatic newborns, with 40% to 58% developing permanent sequelae, including sensorineural hearing loss, ophthalmological deficits, and neurodevelopmental delays. [17] The most common complication of cCMV infection is hearing loss in 35% of symptomatic newborns and 7% to 10% of newborns asymptomatic at birth. [15] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Prognosis
retrieved_docs.content: Prognosis is variable. Symptomatic cCMV leads to long-term sequelae including sensorineural hearing loss, mental and developmental disabilities, and impaired vision. Around 10% of symptomatic newborns will perish in the neonatal period, a number that may be an underestimate. Asymptomatic babies are not spared long-term sequelae, with 10% to 12% developing hearing loss and a smaller percentage developing neuromotor disabilities and vision problems. [17] Infants of mothers who acquired primary CMV in pregnancy or who are symptomatic with microcephaly, intracranial calcifications, or chorioretinitis have the worst prognosis. [43]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Since only about 10% of infected newborns are symptomatic at birth, the disease is difficult to detect at birth. Nevertheless, the live birth prevalence rate in developed countries is 0.6% to 0.7% or more than 1 in 200, resulting in about 40,000 cases annually in the United States. [16] The incidence of cCMV is higher in developing countries, ranging from 1% to 5%.

retrieved_docs.title: Embryology, Teratology TORCH -- Clinical Significance -- CMV:
retrieved_docs.content: Though commonly asymptomatic in healthy adults, CMV affects an estimated 60% of people in the United States (age-adjusted). [32] Additionally, CMV is the number one cause of infection-induced birth defects. Congenital CMV affects one in every 150 live births globally. [33] CMV is also the leading cause of nongenetic congenital hearing loss. Approximately 7 to 10% of infants with congenital CMV develop clinical manifestations. These include petechiae, jaundice, hepatosplenomegaly, chorioretinitis as well as neurological deficits, which consists of physical and mental retardation, deafness, and even death in about 10% of patients. Many of these infants are born asymptomatic and lack proper screening for congenital CMV. They are likely to develop these issues later in life. It is common practice to treat symptomatic CMV positive infants with six months of valganciclovir. Asymptomatic infants customarily receive no treatment. [34] [23] [15] [22]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Complications occur more commonly in symptomatic cCMV infection, including the following: Sensorineural hearing loss is the most common complication, which affects both symptomatic and asymptomatic neonates Neurodevelopmental delays, including motor deficits, eg, cerebral palsy, and cognitive deficits Ophthalmological deficits ranging from the clinical finding of chorioretinitis to optic atrophy that may be associated with vision loss (see Image . Cytomegalovirus Retinitis) Seizures Death

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Cytomegalovirus (CMV) infection is the most common congenital infection worldwide and the leading cause of nonhereditary sensorineural hearing loss in children. [1] [2] Congenital CMV infection (cCMV) is asymptomatic in 85% to 90% of newborns at birth. [3] [4] Symptomatic congenital disease occurs most frequently after primary maternal infection in pregnancy. Although much less common, symptomatic cCMV carries a mortality risk of up to 7% to 12% in the early neonatal period.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Congenital cytomegalovirus (cCMV) infection is the most common congenital infection worldwide and the leading nonhereditary cause of sensorineural hearing loss in children. This condition affects over 1 in 200 live births in developed countries. Although 85% to 90% of newborns with cCMV are asymptomatic at birth, up to 15% of these infants develop long-term sequelae, including progressive hearing loss, neurodevelopmental delays, and visual impairment. Symptomatic infants are at significantly greater risk for morbidity and mortality, often presenting with microcephaly, hepatosplenomegaly, and central nervous system damage. The risk of adverse events is highest following a primary maternal infection during pregnancy. Despite its prevalence and long-term consequences, cCMV remains underrecognized by both women of reproductive age and many healthcare professionals, with awareness lagging behind other congenital conditions that occur less frequently, such as toxoplasmosis or Down syndrome.

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- History and Physical
retrieved_docs.content: In affected newborns, jaundice, petechiae, hepatosplenomegaly, and microcephaly represent the most common clinical signs (see Image . Fetal Microcephaly). [4] [5] [16] [29] [30] Prematurity or growth restriction may also be present. Ophthalmologic manifestations include chorioretinitis, with or without optic atrophy, and cataracts. Hearing loss—a hallmark of cCMV infection—may be present at birth or develop later, underscoring the importance of regular audiologic monitoring in affected children. Laboratory findings may include conjugated hyperbilirubinemia, thrombocytopenia, elevated aspartate aminotransferase levels, and increased cerebrospinal fluid protein. [29] Cranial imaging findings frequently reveal periventricular calcifications, ventricular dilatation, ventricular cysts, and lenticulostriate vasculopathy. [16] [18]

retrieved_docs.title: Congenital Cytomegalovirus Infection -- Epidemiology
retrieved_docs.content: Seroprevalence rates in women of reproductive age are high and differ based on socioeconomic status. In developed countries, the seroprevalence rate ranges from 40% to 83% among women, [13] and in developing countries, it approaches nearly 100%. [14] However, in women of lower socioeconomic status in developed countries, the seroprevalence rate approaches that seen in developing countries. The acquisition of CMV in industrialized nations is typically due to frequent contact with small children. At the same time, in the developing world, transmission occurs early in life through breastfeeding and crowded living conditions. [15]

retrieved_docs.content: and cardiovascular complications are a subset of the overall perinatal condition spectrum. Collectively, these interrelated entities illustrate how gestational age, birthweight, and fetal growth trajectories define a network of classifications—from specific gestational milestones (29‑30 weeks) to broad perinatal disorder groups—providing a comprehensive framework for understanding, diagnosing, and managing health issues in liveborn infants.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1494
retrieved_docs.content: A gestational age of 29‑30 weeks completed is associated with a liveborn infant, indicating that pregnancies reaching this stage can result in a live birth.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 5 -- Community 2944
retrieved_docs.content: In embryologie, the designation “Allowed qualifier” is used to encompass several developmental conditions, specifically عدوى بفيروس معوي (intestinal viral infection), تضيُّق الصمام المترالي (mitral valve stenosis), and فالج (paralysis). This classification indicates that these entities are recognized as permissible qualifiers when describing embryological abnormalities or variations, underscoring the range of potential fetal developmental issues and the need for their identification in medical evaluation and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2034
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both listed as members of the Arabic‑named SMQ “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”, which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This classification indicates that, within this standardized medical terminology, these two distinct clinical categories are considered part of a broader collection of disorders that share underlying developmental, hereditary, or genetic characteristics. Recognizing their inclusion in the same SMQ can aid clinicians and researchers in organizing diagnostic codes, facilitating epidemiological studies, and ensuring comprehensive patient care that addresses both congenital urogenital conditions and infections transmitted through sexual contact under a unified framework.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 14 -- Level 4 -- Community 2873
retrieved_docs.content: The condition referred to as اضطراب جنيني (fetal disorder) is classified as a type of مضاعفات الحمل (pregnancy complication). Recognizing that fetal disorders are encompassed within the broader category of pregnancy complications helps clinicians identify, monitor, and manage these issues during prenatal care, ensuring appropriate assessment and intervention for both maternal and fetal health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 3 -- Community 1558
retrieved_docs.content: Other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions are linked to the concept الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, using this term to describe their pathology. The category الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ) (congenital, familial and genetic disorders) serves as a central grouping that encompasses several distinct conditions: genitourinary congenital anomalies, other hereditary and degenerative nervous system conditions, and sexually transmitted infections. This SMQ grouping is itself classified as a member of الْتِهابُ الدِّماغِ المُنْتَشِرُ حَولَ المحور, indicating that these congenital, familial and genetic disorders are associated with diffuse encephalitic processes around the axis. Additionally, the SMQ group is also a member of فالج طفلي (infantile cerebral palsy), highlighting its relevance to pediatric neurodevelopmental impairment. Together, these relationships illustrate how hereditary, degenerative, congenital, and infectious conditions intersect within broader neuroinflammatory and pediatric neurological frameworks, informing a more integrated understanding of patient diagnosis and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 2 -- Community 1089
retrieved_docs.content: with or lead to infectious complications around birth. Overall, the knowledge network portrays neonatal disorders as an interconnected group of conditions that include traumatic cerebral injury, intracranial and neonatal hemorrhage, perinatal infections, metabolic diseases such as transient neonatal diabetes, neurologic problems like developmental delay and epilepsy, and extreme prematurity. Birth trauma is a key antecedent of intracranial bleeding, which is classified under neonatal hemorrhage, while perinatal infection serves both as a parent condition for unspecified perinatal disorders and as a member of the broader neonatal disorder set. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in comprehensive assessment, diagnosis, and management of newborns presenting with any of these interrelated pathologies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 1 -- Community 41
retrieved_docs.content: and extreme prematurity with unspecified weight (ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}). These entities share parentage with unspecified perinatal conditions, which are children of perinatal infections (عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك) and are also part of the SMQ for unspecified perinatal infections. Collectively, the network demonstrates how preterm gestation, fetal growth restriction, respiratory distress, cerebral injury, diabetes mellitus, varicose veins, ulceration, and related amputation procedures interrelate, providing clinicians with a structured framework for identifying high‑risk infants, anticipating metabolic and vascular sequelae, and guiding preventive and therapeutic strategies to improve perinatal and long‑term outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 1 -- Community 173
retrieved_docs.content: are recognized components of the broader newborn disorder grouping. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical and classificatory framework in which perinatal conditions, specific respiratory/cardiovascular disorders, growth‑restriction syndromes, low‑birth‑weight classifications, and perinatal infections are interrelated, facilitating comprehensive assessment and coordinated care for infants affected by these overlapping medical issues.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 4 -- Community 2768
retrieved_docs.content: Genitourinary congenital anomalies and sexually transmitted infections are both included within the SMQ titled الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ), which groups together congenital, familial and genetic disorders. This SMQ itself is classified as a member of the broader category فجل طفلي, indicating that these conditions are considered part of the larger spectrum of childhood developmental disorders. The hierarchical relationship underscores how specific anomalies and infections are organized under a unified genetic and congenital disorder framework, which in turn is situated within the context of pediatric developmental health.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 1552
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory distress syndrome is classified as a type of اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة (neonatal disorder). The broader category اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة includes the specific conditions كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة. Virologie is an allowed qualifier for اضطراب لدى حديث الولادة, for respiratory distress syndrome, and also for كثرة الأرومات الحمر الجنينية and رضح الولادة, indicating that viral factors may be relevant in the assessment and management of these neonatal disorders. This hierarchical organization links respiratory distress syndrome to other neonatal conditions and emphasizes the potential role of virology across the spectrum of neonatal pathology.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 2 -- Community 1032
retrieved_docs.content: The category “الاضطرابات الخِلقية والعائلية والجينية (SMQ)”—congenital, familial and genetic disorders—includes several distinct conditions. Genitourinary congenital anomalies are classified as members of this SMQ group, as are sexually transmitted infections, which are also considered part of the same broader classification. In addition, infantile cerebral palsy (فالج طفلي) and Stickle syndrome (متلازمة ستيكلر) are each listed as members of the SMQ category. By grouping these diverse entities under the umbrella of congenital, familial, and genetic disorders, the SMQ framework emphasizes their shared etiological themes and supports integrated clinical assessment and management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 2 -- Community 395
retrieved_docs.content: disorder (اضطراب مخي وعائي), reflecting that these sequelae constitute a subset of broader cerebrovascular disorders. Collectively, these hierarchical and cross‑disciplinary relationships organize diagnoses by linking specific entities such as congenital vascular dilatation, late cerebrovascular sequelae, and rectal pathology to their larger disease families, underscoring the importance of integrated surgical, pathophysiological, pathological and virological perspectives in patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 3 -- Community 2106
retrieved_docs.content: فالج is a general medical condition, and فالج طفلي represents a more specific, narrower form of فالج, indicating the infantile variant of this condition.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 2 -- Community 508
retrieved_docs.content: disorder). Clinically, it is included within **اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)**, the neonatal disorders grouping, and is mapped from **حالة غير محددة نشأت في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة** (unspecified condition arising in the perinatal period). This perinatal condition is linked to **عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك** (nonspecific perinatal infection) and has a broader relationship with **تقييد النمو الجنيني** (fetal growth restriction). **Respiratory distress syndrome** is cross‑referenced with **متلازمة خلل نضج الرئة** (pulmonary immaturity syndrome). Within **اضطرابات لدى حديث الولادة (SMQ)**, additional members such as **ولادة بأقصى درجات الابتسار، غير محددة {الوزن}** (extreme prematurity of unspecified weight) and **عدوى في الفترة المحيطة بالولادة، غير محدد بخلاف ذلك** reinforce the connection between neonatal respiratory compromise, extreme prematurity, and perinatal infection. This integrated hierarchy supports comprehensive patient care by aligning gestational
